events.title: Christians Killed in NW Nigeria, Homes Burned in Northeast – ChurchLeaders
events.body:
# Nigeria Faces Dual Christian Attacks as Violence Escalates

Christian communities in Nigeria suffered violent attacks in two separate regions, with believers killed in the northwest and homes destroyed in the northeast, according to ChurchLeaders.

The attacks represent the latest wave of persecution targeting Christians across Africa’s most populous nation. In northwestern Nigeria, assailants killed Christians in what continues a disturbing pattern of violence against religious minorities in the region. The area has seen recurring attacks on Christian communities in recent years.

Meanwhile, in northeastern Nigeria, attackers burned homes belonging to Christians, displacing families and destroying property. ChurchLeaders reported that the destruction of residences represents a significant escalation in tactics aimed at forcing Christians from their communities.

The dual attacks highlight the precarious security situation facing Nigerian Christians, who experience violence across multiple regions of the country. Northwestern states have become particularly dangerous, with armed groups frequently targeting Christian villages and rural communities. The northeast has long faced instability due to extremist insurgencies operating in the region.

These incidents add to the mounting toll of persecution against Christians in Nigeria, where believers regularly face targeted killings, kidnappings, and destruction of property. The attacks underscore the ongoing challenges for religious freedom and the safety of Christian communities throughout Nigeria’s diverse geographical regions.

Authorities have not yet provided information about potential arrests or security measures following these latest attacks.

events.title: Over 30 Christians beheaded in Mozambique, churches burned – mnnonline.org
events.body:
**Mozambique Witnesses Mass Killing of Christians**

More than 30 Christians were beheaded in Mozambique in a brutal attack that also saw churches set ablaze, according to mnnonline.org.

The violent assault targeted the Christian community in the African nation, resulting in the execution-style killings of over three dozen believers. In addition to the mass beheadings, attackers destroyed church buildings by burning them to the ground.

The incident represents one of the deadliest attacks on Christians in Mozambique in recent memory. The country has experienced growing instability in certain regions, where religious minorities have faced increasing threats and violence.

Details about the specific location of the attack, the identity of the perpetrators, and the exact timing of the incident were not provided in the available information. The report from mnnonline.org confirmed the death toll and the destruction of church property but did not elaborate on whether survivors were displaced or if additional community members were affected beyond those killed.

The attack underscores the dangerous conditions facing Christian communities in parts of Mozambique, where religious persecution has escalated. Church buildings serving as centers of worship and community gathering were deliberately targeted alongside the killing of congregants, suggesting a coordinated effort to eliminate Christian presence in the affected area.

The full extent of the damage to the Christian community and any response from local or international authorities remains unclear.

events.title: Anglican pastor’s wife, church member killed in attack during worship, church burned – Christian Post
events.body:
A woman and church member were killed during a violent attack on an Anglican congregation while they worshipped, according to the Christian Post. The woman was the wife of the church’s pastor.

The assault occurred as the congregation gathered for worship services. In addition to the fatal attack on the two victims, the assailants set fire to the church building, destroying it.

The Christian Post reports that the violence specifically targeted the worshipping community, striking during their religious gathering when members were assembled together. The pastor’s wife and another church member lost their lives in the incident.

The attack represents a severe escalation of violence against the Christian community, with perpetrators not only committing murder but also destroying the physical place of worship by burning the church structure. The timing of the assault during active worship services suggests the attackers deliberately chose a moment when the congregation would be gathered together.

Details about the specific location of the attack, the identities of the perpetrators, and whether any other church members sustained injuries were not provided in the available reporting. The incident highlights ongoing threats faced by Christian communities in certain regions where worship gatherings can become targets of violent persecution. The destruction of the church building adds to the trauma experienced by the surviving congregation members.

events.title: China underground church pastors arrested, face up to 3 years in jail, NGO head says – reuters.com
events.body:
**Chinese Underground Church Pastors Face Three Years Imprisonment Following Arrests**

Chinese authorities have arrested multiple pastors from underground churches who now face up to three years in jail, according to an NGO head cited by Reuters.

The arrests target leaders of unregistered house churches in China, which operate outside the government-controlled Three-Self Patriotic Movement. These underground congregations have faced increasing pressure from authorities seeking to bring all religious activities under state supervision.

Reuters reports that the detained pastors could receive sentences of up to three years imprisonment, though specific charges have not been detailed in the available information. The exact number of those arrested and the locations of the detentions remain unspecified.

An NGO head confirmed the arrests and warned of the potential jail terms facing the detained church leaders. Underground churches in China have historically provided worship spaces for Christians who reject government oversight of their religious practices or object to state-approved theological teachings.

The Chinese government maintains that religious activities must be conducted through officially sanctioned channels and has intensified enforcement of regulations governing unregistered religious gatherings in recent years. These latest arrests continue a pattern of action against independent Christian worship communities operating outside state control.

The fate of the arrested pastors remains uncertain as they await potential prosecution under Chinese law governing unauthorized religious activities.

events.title: Pastors and staff from underground church are arrested in China – NPR
events.body:
Chinese authorities have arrested multiple pastors and staff members from an underground church, according to NPR. The raid targeted a house church operating outside the government-approved religious framework in China.

The arrests represent the latest crackdown on unregistered Christian congregations in the country, where religious activities must be conducted under state supervision. Underground churches, also known as house churches, operate independently of the government-controlled Three-Self Patriotic Movement and often face harassment and closure.

The detained church leaders and staff now face potential criminal charges for their involvement in unauthorized religious activities. Chinese law requires all religious organizations to register with authorities and submit to government oversight, including approval of sermon content and religious teachings.

This incident reflects ongoing tensions between China’s Christian community and state religious policies. While the Chinese constitution technically guarantees freedom of religious belief, the government maintains strict control over religious practice and has intensified restrictions on unauthorized gatherings in recent years.

The fate of the arrested individuals remains unclear, as NPR reports. House church members in China have previously faced interrogation, fines, imprisonment, and forced closure of their meeting spaces. The government views unregistered religious activities as potential threats to social stability and Communist Party authority, leading to periodic waves of enforcement against underground congregations throughout the nation.

events.title: 19 Iranian Christians Confirmed Dead Amid Protests – persecution.org
events.body:
# Iranian Christians Among Dead in Recent Protests

At least 19 Iranian Christians have been confirmed dead amid protests in Iran, according to persecution.org. The report does not provide additional details about the circumstances of the deaths, the specific timing of the protests, or the locations where the incidents occurred.

The confirmation of these deaths highlights the dangerous situation facing religious minorities in Iran during periods of civil unrest. However, the source material does not specify whether the believers were targeted specifically for their faith or were casualties of broader protest-related violence.

Iran has long been recognized as one of the most difficult countries for Christians to practice their faith openly. The nation’s Christian community, which includes both ethnic Armenian and Assyrian Christians as well as converts from Islam, has faced increasing pressure in recent years.

The persecution.org report confirms the number of Christian deaths but does not elaborate on individual cases, family members affected, or responses from religious communities within Iran. No information is provided about whether authorities have investigated these deaths or if any accountability measures have been taken.

The lack of detailed information in the source material itself reflects the challenges of documenting religious persecution and casualties during civil unrest in Iran, where independent reporting is severely restricted.

events.title: 21 of 22 Churches Destroyed in Christian Town Since 2021 Coup – persecution.org
events.body:
**Christian Town Devastated as Military Destroys Nearly All Churches**

A predominantly Christian town has seen 21 of its 22 churches destroyed since a military coup in 2021, according to persecution.org. The systematic destruction represents one of the most severe attacks on Christian worship infrastructure in recent years.

The report indicates that only a single church remains standing in the town following three years of military rule. The scale of destruction suggests a coordinated campaign against Christian places of worship rather than isolated incidents.

While persecution.org did not provide specific details about the town’s location or population, the near-total elimination of church buildings has effectively stripped the Christian community of their ability to gather for corporate worship. The timing, beginning with the 2021 military takeover, points to increased religious persecution following the political upheaval.

The destruction of 21 churches over a three-year period averages to approximately seven churches demolished or burned annually. This pattern of sustained attacks on Christian property indicates ongoing hostility toward religious minorities under the current regime.

The report does not specify whether casualties occurred during the church destructions or detail the methods used to destroy the buildings. The fate of the remaining church and the current status of the Christian population also remain unclear from the available information.

This systematic elimination of worship spaces represents a severe restriction on religious freedom for believers in the affected town.

events.title: Three Members of Christian Family in India Killed – persecution.org
events.body:
# Christian Family Attacked in India, Three Members Killed

Three members of a Christian family in India have been killed in a violent attack, according to persecution.org.

The incident adds to growing concerns about violence targeting religious minorities in the South Asian nation. While persecution.org confirmed the deaths of three family members, specific details about the circumstances surrounding the killings, including the exact location within India and the date of the attack, were not provided in available reports.

The attack appears to be part of a broader pattern of hostility faced by Christian communities in various regions of India. Religious minorities, particularly Christians and Muslims, have increasingly reported incidents of violence and intimidation in recent years.

India’s Christian population represents a small minority in the predominantly Hindu nation, yet they have established communities throughout the country. Despite constitutional protections for religious freedom, Christians have faced mounting challenges, including accusations of forced conversions and social ostracism in some areas.

The deaths of these three family members underscore the real dangers faced by some Christian families in India. Local authorities’ response to the incident and whether any arrests have been made remain unclear from available information. The tragedy highlights ongoing concerns about the safety and security of religious minorities in the world’s largest democracy.

events.title: At least 18 Christians Killed in Crackdown of Iran Protests – Christianity Today
events.body:
# Iran Protests Result in Deaths of Christian Participants

At least 18 Christians have been killed during Iran’s crackdown on recent protests, according to Christianity Today. The deaths occurred as Iranian authorities moved to suppress widespread demonstrations across the country.

The report indicates that believers were among those targeted in the government’s response to the unrest. While specific details about the circumstances of each death remain limited, the casualties reflect the broader violence that has accompanied Iran’s efforts to quell dissent.

Christianity Today’s reporting highlights how Christians participated in the protests alongside other Iranians, making them vulnerable to the same violent crackdown faced by other demonstrators. The Islamic Republic has long maintained strict controls over religious minorities, and Christians in Iran often face surveillance and restrictions on their worship activities.

The deaths add to growing concerns about persecution of religious minorities in Iran, where converting from Islam to Christianity is illegal and house churches operate underground to avoid detection. International human rights organizations have repeatedly called attention to Iran’s treatment of Christian communities, particularly those who gather in informal worship settings.

The exact locations and dates of the killings were not specified in the available reporting, though the incidents occurred during the broader period of civil unrest that has challenged Iranian authorities.

events.title: Thirty Christians killed in Israel’s war with Hamas, say church sources – The Catholic Herald
events.body:
At least thirty Christians have been killed during the ongoing conflict between Israel and Hamas, according to church sources cited by The Catholic Herald.

The casualties represent members of Christian communities caught in the crossfire of the war that has engulfed the region. Church sources have been tracking the deaths of believers amid the broader humanitarian crisis unfolding in the conflict zone.

The Catholic Herald reports that these figures come directly from church sources monitoring the situation on the ground, though specific details about the circumstances of individual deaths, their locations, or timing were not provided in the available information.

Christians represent a small minority population in the areas affected by the Israel-Hamas war, and their communities have found themselves vulnerable amid the intense fighting. The reported death toll of thirty believers highlights the impact the conflict has had on religious minorities in the region.

Church sources continue to document the toll on Christian populations as the war progresses, though comprehensive information about casualties remains difficult to obtain given the challenging conditions and ongoing violence. The figure of thirty deaths underscores the human cost of the conflict extending beyond the primary combatants to affect civilian populations of various faiths caught in the midst of the hostilities.

events.title: 35 Nigerian Christians Killed, Nearly 175 Others Abducted In Separate Attacks – religionunplugged.com
events.body:
# Nigerian Christians Face Deadly Attacks and Mass Abductions

At least 35 Nigerian Christians have been killed and nearly 175 others abducted in separate attacks, according to Religion Unplugged. The incidents represent a severe escalation of violence targeting Christian communities in the region.

The attacks occurred across multiple locations, though the specific timing and geographic details of each incident were not immediately specified in the initial reports. The mass abduction of approximately 175 individuals marks one of the larger-scale kidnapping operations targeting Christians in recent months.

Nigeria has long struggled with violence against Christian communities, particularly in the northern and central regions where militant groups and armed herders have carried out raids on villages and religious gatherings. These latest incidents add to a disturbing pattern of persecution that has claimed thousands of lives and displaced countless families over the past several years.

The coordinated nature of the separate attacks suggests organized targeting of Christian populations. Mass abductions have become an increasingly common tactic, with victims often held for ransom or subjected to forced conversion attempts.

Religious freedom advocates have repeatedly called for increased security measures and international attention to the crisis facing Nigerian Christians. The country remains one of the most dangerous places in the world for Christian communities, with attacks occurring with alarming frequency despite government promises to address the violence.

events.title: Catholic Authorities: Over 100 Christians Killed, 90,000 Displaced in Nigeria – persecution.org
events.body:
# Nigeria Violence Leaves Over 100 Christians Dead, Thousands Displaced

Catholic authorities in Nigeria have reported a devastating toll of violence against Christian communities, with over 100 believers killed and approximately 90,000 people displaced from their homes, according to persecution.org.

The figures represent a significant humanitarian crisis affecting Christian populations in the West African nation, where religious violence has escalated in recent years. The displacement of 90,000 individuals indicates entire communities have been forced to flee their homes, leaving behind property, livelihoods, and ancestral lands.

Catholic church leaders who compiled the data have documented the deadly attacks, though specific details about the timeframe, locations of the violence, and identities of perpetrators were not provided in the report. The scale of displacement suggests widespread and systematic targeting of Christian areas, creating a large population of internally displaced persons requiring humanitarian assistance.

Nigeria has long struggled with religious tensions and violence affecting both Christian and Muslim communities, particularly in the country’s Middle Belt region where farming communities and herders frequently clash. The Catholic authorities’ documentation of these casualties provides crucial evidence of the ongoing security challenges facing religious minorities.

The report underscores the severe persecution facing Christians in parts of Nigeria, where violence continues to claim lives and uproot families from their communities, creating a growing refugee crisis within the nation’s borders.

events.title: Thousands of Christian Families Face Eviction in Pakistan – persecution.org
events.body:
# Thousands of Christian Families Face Eviction Crisis in Pakistan

Thousands of Christian families in Pakistan are facing the threat of eviction, according to a report from persecution.org. The alarming situation represents a significant challenge for the Christian minority community in the South Asian nation.

While specific details about the location and circumstances surrounding these potential evictions remain limited in available reporting, the scale of the crisis is substantial, affecting thousands of households across the country. The situation highlights ongoing vulnerabilities faced by religious minorities in Pakistan, where Christians constitute a small percentage of the predominantly Muslim population.

The threatened evictions could potentially displace a significant number of believers, leaving them without homes and forcing families into precarious living situations. Such large-scale displacement would represent a serious humanitarian concern for the affected community.

Pakistan has long been identified as a difficult environment for Christian minorities, who frequently face various forms of discrimination and pressure. Housing insecurity adds another layer of vulnerability to communities already navigating complex social and legal challenges.

The full impact of these threatened evictions on the Christian population remains to be seen, though advocacy groups continue to monitor the situation closely. The crisis underscores the ongoing need for protection of religious minorities and their property rights in regions where they face systematic disadvantages.

events.title: Pakistani Christian Dies After Alleged Torture – persecution.org
events.body:
# Pakistani Christian Dies Following Alleged Torture

A Christian man in Pakistan has died following what his family alleges was torture while in custody, according to persecution.org.

The man reportedly died after experiencing severe mistreatment. His family maintains that he was subjected to torture, though specific details about the circumstances of his detention or the nature of the alleged abuse were not provided in the available reporting.

Pakistan remains one of the world’s most challenging environments for Christians, who constitute a small minority in the predominantly Muslim nation. Members of the Christian community frequently face discrimination, false accusations, and violence.

The incident adds to growing concerns about the treatment of religious minorities in Pakistan, where Christians often find themselves vulnerable to abuse and have limited recourse to justice. Blasphemy laws in the country are frequently weaponized against Christians, sometimes leading to mob violence or prolonged detention under harsh conditions.

While the full details surrounding this man’s death remain unclear, the case highlights the ongoing dangers faced by Pakistani Christians. The family’s allegations of torture underscore the precarious situation many believers navigate daily, where their faith can make them targets of persecution.

The report from persecution.org did not specify whether authorities have launched an investigation into the death or the family’s torture allegations.

events.title: At least 53 killed in Palm Sunday attacks in Christian areas of Nigeria – Baptist Press
events.body:
# Nigeria Palm Sunday Violence Claims Dozens of Lives

At least 53 people were killed in attacks targeting Christian communities in Nigeria on Palm Sunday, according to Baptist Press. The violence struck multiple Christian areas during one of the most significant days in the Christian calendar, when believers commemorate Jesus Christ’s entry into Jerusalem.

The attacks occurred as Christians gathered for Palm Sunday services and observances, a holy day that marks the beginning of Holy Week leading up to Easter. The assault on worshippers during this sacred time represents a particularly severe form of religious persecution.

Nigeria has experienced ongoing violence against Christian communities, with believers frequently facing deadly attacks while gathering for worship or living in predominantly Christian regions. The country’s Christian population has endured systematic targeting, with churches and Christian-majority areas becoming vulnerable sites for coordinated assaults.

The death toll of at least 53 victims underscores the scale of this Palm Sunday violence, though Baptist Press indicates the number could represent a minimum count. Such attacks during major Christian celebrations have become a recurring pattern in the region, forcing believers to worship under the constant threat of violence.

The incident adds to the growing crisis of Christian persecution in Nigeria, where communities of faith face regular threats to their safety and ability to practice their religion freely.

events.title: Ethiopian Church faces a rise in persecution – mnnonline.org
events.body:
# Ethiopian Church Experiences Increased Persecution

The Ethiopian Church is facing a rise in persecution, according to a report from mnnonline.org. While specific details about the nature and extent of the persecution were limited in the available information, the report indicates a troubling trend for Christians in the East African nation.

Ethiopia has historically been home to one of the world’s oldest Christian communities, with the Ethiopian Orthodox Church dating back to the fourth century. The country’s Christian population has long coexisted with other religious groups, though tensions have periodically emerged throughout its history.

The report’s mention of rising persecution suggests that conditions for believers may be deteriorating, though the source material did not provide specific incidents, statistics, or details about what forms the persecution is taking. The lack of detailed information in the available source prevents a fuller understanding of whether the persecution stems from government authorities, extremist groups, or interfaith conflicts.

Without additional context from the source material, it remains unclear which Christian denominations are most affected, what geographic regions are experiencing the most severe incidents, or what factors are driving the reported increase in persecution. The situation warrants continued monitoring as developments unfold in Ethiopia’s complex religious landscape.

events.title: Baptist denomination banned in Nicaragua as religious persecution grows, CSW reports – Baptist Press
events.body:
# Nicaragua Bans Baptist Denomination Amid Growing Religious Persecution

Nicaragua has officially banned a Baptist denomination, marking another escalation in the country’s mounting campaign against religious organizations, according to a report by Christian Solidarity Worldwide.

The ban represents the latest action in what monitoring groups describe as intensifying religious persecution within the Central American nation. CSW, an international human rights organization specializing in religious freedom advocacy, documented this development as part of a broader pattern of government restrictions on Christian communities.

The Baptist Press reports that the prohibition comes amid growing concerns about the systematic targeting of faith-based institutions in Nicaragua. While specific details about which Baptist denomination faced the ban were included in the original reporting, the action reflects the government’s increasing willingness to formally dissolve religious organizations.

Religious freedom advocates have observed a disturbing trend of state interference with Christian churches and ministries throughout Nicaragua in recent years. The banning of an entire denomination represents a significant escalation beyond previous measures, which often involved property seizures, intimidation, or administrative obstacles.

CSW’s documentation of this case adds to mounting evidence of coordinated efforts to suppress religious activities in the country. The organization continues monitoring the situation as concerns grow about the safety and legal standing of other Christian groups operating in Nicaragua.

This development underscores the precarious position of religious communities facing authoritarian pressure in the region.

events.title: Attacks Rock Suqaylabiyah, a Christian Town in Hama, Syria
events.body:
Scores of men from a Sunni town attacked a predominantly Christian town in central Syria, causing widespread damage to homes, shops and cars, according to Coptic Solidarity. The attacks in Suqaylabiyah, located in Hama province, lasted until the early hours of Saturday and reportedly followed an argument between two men.

During the assault, scores of men on motorcycles from the nearby Sunni town of Qalaat al-Madiq targeted Christian property. “We passed through a state of terror, fear, and panic,” said a man whose clothes shop was riddled with bullets and heavily damaged. He added that his children were terrified during the hours-long attack.

The man explained that an argument between a resident of Suqaylabiyah and another from Qalaat al-Madiq led to scores of men from the Sunni town storming the area. “It is unfair that because of an argument two towns clashed,” he stated.

Another resident reported that young men broke into his house and kicked a diesel heater, setting a room ablaze. They attempted to ignite another room but failed after a neighbor intervened, who was then struck with a stick by one of the attackers.

Government forces deployed reinforcements to calm the violence. Hundreds of residents marched through the streets Saturday demanding accountability and declaring a strike until perpetrators face punishment. The incident highlights ongoing targeting of Syria’s Christian minority.

events.title: Syrian Christians Cancel Holy Week Events After Attacks, Church Leaders Warn of Escalating Threats
events.body:
Christian communities across Syria canceled Holy Week events in April 2026 following violent attacks on Christian towns and religious symbols, according to Coptic Solidarity.

The most serious incidents occurred in Al-Suqaylabiyah, a predominantly Christian town in Hama countryside, where groups of attackers launched coordinated assaults on residents and property. Violence reportedly began at a liquor store before escalating as radicalized young men from nearby areas looted shops and destroyed a statue of the Virgin Mary in a public square. Some attackers allegedly attempted to assault young women and threatened residents with further violence.

A Syriac Catholic archbishop described assailants arriving in large numbers, armed and on motorcycles, firing shots into the air and creating a climate of fear and terror. Critically, he alleged that members of security forces and police were among the attackers, and authorities failed to respond promptly to protect the community.

In Tartus province, attackers vandalized a Christian cemetery in Al-Rawda, smashing crosses on tombstones.

Following these incidents, churches in Damascus, Aleppo, and areas surrounding Al-Suqaylabiyah canceled Palm Sunday processions and limited Holy Week observances to indoor prayers. Church leaders in Aleppo framed cancellations as collective responses to violence rather than isolated precautions.

The Syrian government, controlled by jihadist group Hayat Tahrir al-Sham since late 2024, publicly claimed to ensure church protection, though accounts from church leaders suggest a disconnect between official assurances and ground conditions.

events.title: China targets lawyers defending jailed Christian leaders
events.body:
Chinese authorities are intensifying pressure on lawyers defending church leaders from a prominent Protestant congregation in Beijing, according to ChinaAid. Nearly 20 people associated with the church, including its founder, were arrested in October in one of China’s largest crackdowns on Christians in recent years.

The arrested pastor had led efforts to operate outside the government’s tightly controlled system of official churches and had expanded his ministry online in recent years, drawing increased scrutiny from authorities before his arrest.

Since the October arrests, lawyers defending the congregation have faced mounting threats. Authorities revoked the legal license of one lawyer involved in the case, while several others have had their licenses suspended or received verbal warnings during recent meetings with officials, according to the pastor’s daughter and another lawyer familiar with the situation.

Representatives of the church stated in a letter this week that they “feel deeply that this is an open defiance and trampling of justice and the rule of law.” China’s Ministry of Justice did not respond to requests for comment.

The targeting of defense lawyers could make it harder for the pastor’s family to obtain information about his condition and mount an effective legal defense, according to his daughter. U.S. Secretary of State and members of Congress have called for the pastor’s release, underscoring international concern over the case.

events.title: ISIS Affiliates Threaten Religious Freedom in the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC)
events.body:
ISIS affiliates are mounting an intensifying threat against religious freedom in the Democratic Republic of Congo, according to a report released by the U.S. Commission on International Religious Freedom.

The Allied Democratic Forces, also known as Islamic State – Democratic Republic of Congo, has escalated violent operations over the past two years in the nation’s eastern provinces. The group has targeted houses of worship, religious leaders, and communities that are predominantly Christian, according to USCIRF.

The commission’s report examines how nonstate armed groups exploit ongoing political instability and chronic insecurity in the DRC. These actors take advantage of gaps in governance and limited civilian protection to increasingly target religious communities, particularly in eastern areas of the country.

USCIRF’s publication provides an overview of the Allied Democratic Forces’ violations of freedom of religion or belief, alongside those committed by other nonstate actors. The report also analyzes how the DRC government and international community have responded to the crisis.

In its 2026 Annual Report, USCIRF documented religious freedom violations connected to ISIS affiliates operating in central, eastern, and southern Africa. The commission has previously examined conditions in the central region of the continent, including in a February 2023 report on the area.

The escalating violence underscores the growing challenge of protecting religious communities in regions where armed groups operate beyond government control.

events.title: USCIRF Releases New Report about Religious Tensions and Fulani Communities in West and Central Africa
events.body:
The United States Commission on International Religious Freedom released a report in October 2020 examining religious tensions involving Fulani communities across West and Central Africa, according to information published by USCIRF.

The factsheet explores how religion contributes to escalating violence both committed by and against Fulani communities in the region. Predominantly Muslim and historically associated with cattle herding and livestock rearing, Fulani communities represent one of the largest ethnic groups globally, stretching across the African continent from Senegal to Sudan.

According to the USCIRF report, these communities have been both victims and perpetrators of violence against civilians in multiple countries in recent years. While debate continues regarding the extent to which religious ideology drives this violence, the commission noted that increasing violence involving Fulani groups is clearly aggravating religious tensions in countries including Nigeria and the Central African Republic.

The factsheet was released as part of USCIRF’s broader examination of religious freedom conditions internationally. The commission acknowledged that although the precise role of religious factors remains contested, the trend of escalating violence connected to Fulani communities has become a significant concern for religious freedom and interfaith relations across the region.

USCIRF’s analysis highlights the complex intersection of ethnicity, livelihood patterns, and religious identity contributing to tensions affecting both Fulani communities and surrounding populations throughout West and Central Africa.

events.title: Thousands of Christian Families Face Eviction in Pakistan
events.body:
# **Thousands of Christian Families Threatened with Eviction in Pakistan**

Approximately 25,000 Christians living in three colonies in Islamabad, Pakistan, are facing imminent eviction after authorities ordered them to leave their homes within days, according to International Christian Concern.

Many of these families were originally settled in the Rimsha, Allama Iqbal, and Akram Masih Gill colonies by the government following a case involving a girl who was falsely accused, which created serious threats against the Christian community. The families were relocated to these areas for their safety and have been building their lives there for years.

However, authorities are now demanding they vacate without offering a proper resettlement plan, alternative land, or clear compensation. The Capital Development Authority claims the settlements are illegal and part of urban development plans, though critics question evicting families that authorities themselves originally placed there.

Christian leaders have organized protests and prayer gatherings, urging the government to halt the evictions and provide fair solutions. They are escalating the matter to higher governmental levels.

Daily life has been severely disrupted for these predominantly low-income families who work in sanitation, domestic service, and day labor. Many are not reporting to work, fearing their homes will be demolished in their absence. Children are missing school as families remain home, anxious about their uncertain future. The fear of losing their homes outweighs even the financial hardship of missing wages.

events.title: Ukrainian Pastor Exiled from Russian-Controlled Region
events.body:
Russian authorities have ordered a 66-year-old pastor to leave the occupied Luhansk region of Ukraine within two weeks, according to International Christian Concern. Officials issued the March 21 eviction notice, claiming the pastor led church services without permission.

When the pastor asked where he should relocate, Russian officials suggested Poland. After he questioned what would happen if he refused to leave, authorities responded they would punish him and remove him by force. The following day, officials demanded he surrender his residence permit for cancellation, but he declined.

The confrontation came ten days after authorities initially challenged the pastor about holding services without government approval. Despite the threats, the pastor and his wife have remained steadfast, refusing to abandon the community he has served for three decades. The pastor stated that the Lord appointed him to serve in Krasnodon.

The pastor leads a church affiliated with the Council of Churches Baptists, a coalition that does not seek official registration in any country. Russian authorities have targeted multiple ministers in recent years, fining them for “illegal missionary activity.”

Russian forces have illegally occupied approximately one-fifth of Ukraine’s territory since March 2025. A report by Mission Eurasia published earlier this year revealed that Russian forces have damaged or destroyed at least 737 religious buildings since the 2022 full-scale invasion began, with about 450 being Baptist churches.

events.title: Pakistani Christian Dies After Alleged Torture
events.body:
A 22-year-old Christian man was found dead at a farmhouse in Punjab, Pakistan, in what his family believes was a murder disguised as suicide, according to International Christian Concern.

The man’s body was discovered on March 3, hanging from a beam at a farmhouse in Sargodha. While the farm owners initially claimed he took his own life, his family discovered extensive injuries and torture marks when preparing the body for burial.

The man had been working at the farm for several years to repay a loan of 270,000 Pakistani rupees ($968) his family had borrowed from the owners. Unable to repay the debt, he was forced into bonded labor, earning only 15,000 rupees ($54) monthly—making it nearly impossible to settle the obligation.

His brother reported that the farm owners frequently beat him and permitted only one annual family visit. During his last visit in November, the man told his family he didn’t want to return, describing severe mistreatment and beatings. However, lacking alternative employment and still owing the debt, he had no choice but to go back.

Following his death, nearly 200 locals protested and blocked roads demanding justice. Police subsequently detained two farm owners and opened an investigation, though authorities also filed charges against 19 Christian protesters for blocking traffic.

Activists note this case highlights Pakistan’s broader bonded labor problem, where religious minorities often become trapped in cycles of debt and abuse, with powerful perpetrators frequently escaping justice.

events.title: Dozens Killed During Palm Sunday Attacks in Nigeria
events.body:
# Nigeria: Deadly Palm Sunday Attacks Leave Dozens Dead

Gunmen killed at least 30 people during a Palm Sunday attack in Ungwan Rukuba, a community in Jos North Local Government Area of Plateau state, Nigeria, according to International Christian Concern.

The attack occurred during the evening when armed men entered the community and opened fire on residents. Eyewitnesses described the assault as coordinated, with multiple casualties and several homes affected.

Following the incident, the Plateau state government imposed a 48-hour curfew in parts of northern Jos. Despite the restriction, residents and youth protested the killings, blocking roads in some areas.

Separate reports from nearby locations including Angwa Rukuba Junction and Eto Baba indicate at least 10 people were killed during gunfire earlier that Sunday. A humanitarian worker stated in a video on social media that the victims were believers attacked on Palm Sunday.

Residents gave differing accounts of the attackers, with one witness describing them as members of Boko Haram and another alleging they were armed Fulani militia who arrived on motorcycles and retreated toward nearby mountainous areas. Security agencies had not issued an official statement confirming the attackers’ identity.

In a separate incident in Kaduna state, gunmen killed at least 13 people during an early morning attack on Kahir village while victims attended a gathering. A resident reported several family members were among those killed, with victims ranging from 21 to 31 years old.
